concepts.title: Properties of Thermal Radiation
concepts.conceptBody:
Properties of Thermal Radiation

  • Radiation involves heat transfer through EM waves
  • Medium is not required
  • At all temp., all bodies continuously emit heat energy through radiations.

concepts.title: Emissive Power
concepts.conceptBody:
Emissive Power

The radiant energy emitted per unit time per unit surface area of the body is called emissive power and is denoted by $\text{E}$.

$\text{E}=\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ U}}{\text{A}\left( \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t} \right)}$

units : W/m ${{~}^{2}}$

The radiant energy is emitted in all wave length range from 0 to $\infty $, however, only a small range corresponds to larger part of the radiant energy.

concepts.title: Spectral Emissive Power
concepts.conceptBody:
Spectral Emissive Power $\left( {{\mathbf{E}}_{\lambda }} \right)$

The radiant energy emitted per unit time, per unit area per unit wavelength is called spectral emissive power. - In the other words, the emissive power for a particular wavelength is called spectral emissive power $\left( {{\text{E}}_{\lambda }} \right)$.

${{\text{E}}_{\lambda }}=\left( \frac{\text{d}E}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}\lambda } \right)$

or $E=\int _{0}^{\infty }\left( {{E}_{\lambda }}d\lambda \right)\equiv $ total emissive power

concepts.title: Absorptive Power
concepts.conceptBody:
Absorptive Power

The fraction of incident radiant energy absorbed by the surface of the body is called absorptive power.

$\text{a}=\frac{\text{ absorbed }\text{ incident }\text{ radiant }\text{ energy }}{\text{ total }\text{ incident }\text{ radiant }\text{ energy }}$

for a perfect black body, $\text{a}=1$

for a perfect white body, $a=0$

for a grey body, $0<\text{a}<1$

Note : The incident radiant energy on the surface of the body is divided into 3 parts :

(i) Absorbed part, (ii) Transmitted part (iii) Reflected

Also, $\alpha +\beta +\gamma =1$ for anybody having incident radiant energy where $\alpha \equiv $ absorptivity, $\beta $ $\equiv $ transitivity, $\gamma \equiv $ reflectivity

concepts.title: Spectral Absorptive Power
concepts.conceptBody:
Spectral Absorptive Power $\left( {{\text{a}}_{\lambda }} \right)$ 

The absorptive power for a particular wavelength is called spectral absorptive power.

\(\begin{array}{l}a_\lambda=\left(\frac{d a}{d \lambda}\right) \\ \Rightarrow a=\int_0^{\infty} a_\lambda d \lambda \equiv \text { total absorptive power } \\ a \equiv \text { area under the } a_\lambda-\lambda \text { curve }\end{array}\)

concepts.title: Kirchhoff's Law
concepts.conceptBody:
Kirchhoff’s Law

It states that

(i) At a given temperature, the ratio of emissive power to the absorptive power is same for all bodies.

(ii) and is equal to the emissive power of the black body at that temperature.

$\left( \frac{{{\text{E}}_{\text{b}}}}{{{\text{a}}_{\text{b}}}} \right)=\left( {{\text{E}}_{\text{bb}}} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \frac{{{\text{E}}_{\text{b}}}}{{{\text{E}}_{\text{bb}}}} \right)={{\text{a}}_{\text{b}}}$

Also, $\frac{{{\left( {{\text{E}}_{\text{b}}} \right)}_{\lambda }}}{{{\left( {{\text{a}}_{\text{b}}} \right)}_{\lambda }}}={{\left( {{\text{E}}_{\text{bb}}} \right)}_{\lambda }}$

In conclusion : "A good emitter is a good absorber and vice-versa".

Proof : 

Consider two bodies, one grey and the other a perfect black body, enclosed in an isolated container, which have identical dimension and are in thermal equilibrium

In thermal equilibrium,

Amount of radiant energy falling on black body is completely reflected black ( $\because $ its temperature is constant)

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{E}}_{\text{bb}}}=\frac{\text{U}}{\text{A}\left( \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t} \right)},{{\text{E}}_{\text{b}}}=\frac{{{\text{a}}_{\text{b}}}\text{U}}{\text{A}\left( \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t} \right)}$ [Energy emitted by body its temperature is constant $]$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{E}}_{\text{b}}}={{\text{a}}_{\text{b}}}{{\text{E}}_{\text{bb}}}$

$\left( \frac{{{\text{E}}_{\text{b}}}}{{{\text{a}}_{\text{b}}}} \right)={{\text{E}}_{\text{bb}}}$

concepts.title: Black Body Radiation
concepts.conceptBody:
Black Body Radiation

On the base of quantum theory of radiation, plank has shown that the energy distribution in the spectrum of black body radiation is given by : ${{R}_{\lambda }}=\frac{2\pi h{{c}^{2}}}{{{\lambda }^{5}}}\left[ \frac{1}{{{e}^{\text{ch}/\lambda \text{KT}}}-1} \right]$ where ${{R}_{\lambda }}\equiv $ spectral radiancy or spectral intensity for a given wavelength. (Radiancy is the intensity due to radiation) 

$\text{T}\equiv $ absolute temp; $\text{h}\equiv $ Planck’s constant 

$\text{K}\equiv $ Boltzmann const $=1.38\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{10}^{23}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ J}/\text{k}$ 

$\text{C}\equiv $ speed of light 

(i) The area under the graph curve, gives the total intensity of radiation, (radiancy) at a particular temperature.

$R=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\infty }{{R}_{\lambda }}d\lambda $

And, this area increases with the increase in temperature.

(ii) Radiancy is found to be proportional to the fourth power of absolute temperature of the surface of the body. (Stefan's law)

$\text{R}\propto {{\text{T}}^{4}}$

$\text{R}=\sigma {{\text{T}}^{4}}$

$\text{U}=\left( \sigma \text{A}{{\text{T}}^{4}} \right)$

concepts.title: Wien's Law
concepts.conceptBody:
Wein's Law

The wavelength for maximum intensity of radiation is inversely proportional to the temp. of the surface

$\begin{matrix} {} & {{\lambda }_{\text{m}}}\propto \frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}} \\ {} & {{\lambda }_{\text{m}}}\text{T}=\text{b }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (Wein }\!\!'\!\!\text{ s }\!\!~\!\!\text{ constant) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ {} & \text{b}=2.88\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{10}^{-3}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}-\text{K} \\ \end{matrix}$

Wein's law can be used to determine the temperature of any surface emitting the radiation.

concepts.title: Stefan's Law
concepts.conceptBody:
Stefan's Law

The radiant energy emitted per unit time by the surface of the body is given by

$\text{u}=\text{e}\sigma \text{A}{{\text{T}}^{4}}$

$A\equiv $ total surface area of the body exposed to the surrounding

$\text{T}\equiv $ absolute temp. of the surface of the body

\(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{e} \equiv \text { emissitivity of the surface } \\ & \sigma=\text { Stefan's constant } \\ & =5.6710^{-8} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^2-\mathrm{K}^4 \end{aligned}\)

Note : 

(1) Emissivity of a black body $=1$

Also, emissivity of any body = absorptive power of the body

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \frac{{{\text{E}}_{\text{b}}}}{{{\text{E}}_{\text{bb}}}}=\left( {{\text{a}}_{\text{b}}} \right) \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \frac{\text{E}\left( \sigma \text{A}{{\text{T}}^{4}} \right)}{\left( \sigma \text{A}{{\text{T}}^{4}} \right)}={{\text{a}}_{\text{b}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ E}=\left( {{\text{a}}_{\text{b}}} \right) \\ \end{array}$

(2) The heat energy lost per unit time by a body ${{t}_{0}}$ is given by :

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ u}=\text{e}\sigma \text{A}\left( {{\text{T}}^{4}}-\text{T}_{0}^{4} \right)$

where $\text{T}\equiv $ temp. of the body

${{\text{T}}_{0}}\equiv $ temp. of the surrounding

$\text{A}\equiv $ Area of the body exposed to the surrounding

$\left( \text{T}>{{\text{T}}_{0}} \right)$

Similarly heat gained per unit time from the surrounding will be given by $=\text{e}\sigma \text{A}\left( {{\text{T}}^{4}}-\text{T}_{0}^{4} \right)$

concepts.title: Newton's Law of Cooling
concepts.conceptBody:
Newton's Cooling Law 

$\begin{array}{l} \Delta\text{ T}=\left( \text{T}-{{\text{T}}_{0}} \right) \\ \Delta\text{ T}<<{{\text{T}}_{0}}\left( \text{in }\text{ K} \right) \\ \end{array}$

Heat const. per unit time due to convection,

$\Delta{{\text{u}}_{1}}=\text{hA}\left( \text{T}-{{\text{T}}_{0}} \right)$

Heat lost per unit time due to radiation

$\Delta \mathrm{u}_2=\operatorname{e\sigma } \mathrm{A}\left(\mathrm{T}^4-\mathrm{T}_0^4\right)$

$=e\sigma \mathrm{A} \mathrm{T}_0^4\left[\left(1+\frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}_0}\right)^4-1\right]$

$\therefore \quad \Delta \mathrm{T} \ll<\mathrm{T}_0$

$=e \sigma \mathrm{AT}_0^4\left[1+\frac{4 \Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}_0}-1\right]$

$=\left(4 \mathrm{e} \sigma \mathrm{AT}_0^3\right)(\Delta \mathrm{T})$

Total heat loss per unit time

$\Delta \mathrm{u}=\Delta \mathrm{u}_1+\Delta \mathrm{u}_2$

$=\left(\mathrm{h}+4 \mathrm{e} \sigma \mathrm{AT}_0^3\right) \mathrm{A}\left(\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{T}_0\right)$

$-\mathrm{ms}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dT}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)=\left(\mathrm{h}+4 \mathrm{e} \sigma \mathrm{AT}_0^3\right) \mathrm{A}\left(\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{T}_0\right)$

$\therefore-\left(\frac{\mathrm{dT}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{h}+4 \mathrm{e} \sigma \mathrm{AT}_0^3}{\mathrm{ms}}\right) \mathrm{A}\left(\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{T}_0\right)$

$-\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} T}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)=\mathrm{K}\left(\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{T}_0\right) \text { (in Kelvin) }$

$\mathrm{K} \equiv \text { cooling constant }$

$=\left(\frac{\mathrm{h}+4 \mathrm{e} \sigma \mathrm{T}_0^3}{\mathrm{ms}}\right) \mathrm{A}$

$\left(\frac{d \theta}{d t}\right)=K\left(\theta-\theta_0\right) \text { in celsius }$

$\int_{\theta i}^\theta \frac{-d \theta}{\theta-\theta_0}=K \int_0^t d t$

$\frac{\ln \left(\theta-\theta_0\right)}{\left(\theta_{\mathrm{i}}-\theta_0\right)}=-\mathrm{Kt}$

$\theta=\theta_0+\left(\theta_{\mathrm{i}}-\theta_0\right) \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{Kt}}$

concepts.title: Cooling Curve
concepts.conceptBody:
Cooling Curve

$\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)=-\tan \alpha$

$=-\mathrm{K}\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$

$\therefore \quad \tan \alpha=\mathrm{K}\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$

$\therefore \quad K=\frac{\tan \alpha}{\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)}$

$\therefore \quad$to calculate the value of $\mathrm{K}$, use the tangent of the slope at point and the temp. at that instant.

examples.title: For a perfectly black body, its absorptive power is

Certain substance emits only the wavelengths $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$ and $\lambda_4$ when it is at a high temperature. When this substance is at a colder temperature, it will absorb only the following wavelengths
